main_paper.raw: {"methods":["WASUP: an inherently interpretable neural network for classification with weight-input alignment and class-discriminative support vectors","Extraction of class-representative support feature vectors from training images","Classification via similarity computation between support vectors and test image latent vectors","Use of B-cos architecture to enable faithful local and global explanations in the input space"],"problems":["Lack of faithful, inherently interpretable explanations in deep learning image classification models","Existing posthoc explainability methods introduce approximation errors for high-dimensional inputs like images","Current inherently interpretable models lack one or more of: local explanations, global explanations, or faithfulness of explanations","Complexity and lack of faithfulness in prototypical part-based explanations"],"datasets":["Stanford Dogs (fine-grained single-label image classification)","RSNA (pathology prediction)","Pascal VOC (multi-label image classification)"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy","Faithfulness of explanations (axiom-based evaluation)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["WASUP is the first inherently interpretable neural network to provide faithful local and global explanations for image classification tasks, fulfilling all three properties (local, global, faithful) simultaneously.","WASUP reduces the complexity of explanations to be evaluated compared to prototypical part-based models.","WASUP explanations are proven to fulfill axioms required for faithfulness, not just empirically but theoretically.","WASUP can be implemented with any neural network feature extractor architecture, including ResNets, Transformers, and Mamba, without limiting computational capacity.","WASUP enables direct and faithful explanations in the input space, allowing intuitive understanding and model debugging."]}

methods: WASUP: an inherently interpretable neural network for classification with weight-input alignment and class-discriminative support vectors
Extraction of class-representative support feature vectors from training images
Classification via similarity computation between support vectors and test image latent vectors
Use of B-cos architecture to enable faithful local and global explanations in the input space
problems: Lack of faithful, inherently interpretable explanations in deep learning image classification models
Existing posthoc explainability methods introduce approximation errors for high-dimensional inputs like images
Current inherently interpretable models lack one or more of: local explanations, global explanations, or faithfulness of explanations
Complexity and lack of faithfulness in prototypical part-based explanations
novelty_claims: WASUP is the first inherently interpretable neural network to provide faithful local and global explanations for image classification tasks, fulfilling all three properties (local, global, faithful) simultaneously.
WASUP reduces the complexity of explanations to be evaluated compared to prototypical part-based models.
WASUP explanations are proven to fulfill axioms required for faithfulness, not just empirically but theoretically.
WASUP can be implemented with any neural network feature extractor architecture, including ResNets, Transformers, and Mamba, without limiting computational capacity.
WASUP enables direct and faithful explanations in the input space, allowing intuitive understanding and model debugging.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["B-cos transformation (a novel input-dependent linear transformation) as a drop-in replacement for linear layers in DNNs","Integration of B-cos transformation into existing architectures (ResNets, DenseNets, ConvNeXt, Vision Transformers, VGGs, ResNeXt)","Combining B-cos with normalisation layers to retain optimisation benefits","Combining B-cos with attention mechanisms in Vision Transformers"],"problems":["Improving the interpretability of deep neural networks (CNNs and Vision Transformers)","Providing explanations that are both faithful to model computations and human-interpretable","Achieving high interpretability without sacrificing model accuracy"],"datasets":["ImageNet","CIFAR10"],"metrics":["Top-1 accuracy","Quantitative interpretability metrics (not named specifically, but referenced as standard in the field)"],"results":[{"metric":"Top-1 accuracy (ImageNet, B-cos models)","value":">80%"},{"metric":"Top-1 accuracy (CIFAR10, plain B-cos CNN)","value":"competitive with standard models"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of the B-cos transformation, which promotes weight-input alignment and enables inherently interpretable DNNs.","B-cos transformation allows any sequence of such layers to be summarised by a single, interpretable linear transformation.","B-cos can be integrated into a wide range of existing architectures, including both CNNs and Vision Transformers, without loss of accuracy.","B-cos explanations outperform other explanation methods under quantitative interpretability metrics and qualitative inspection.","The framework allows for explanations not only of model outputs but also of intermediate representations.","The work extends previous research by integrating normalisation and attention layers into B-cos DNNs and providing broader experimental evaluation."]}

methods: B-cos transformation (a novel input-dependent linear transformation) as a drop-in replacement for linear layers in DNNs
Integration of B-cos transformation into existing architectures (ResNets, DenseNets, ConvNeXt, Vision Transformers, VGGs, ResNeXt)
Combining B-cos with normalisation layers to retain optimisation benefits
Combining B-cos with attention mechanisms in Vision Transformers
problems: Improving the interpretability of deep neural networks (CNNs and Vision Transformers)
Providing explanations that are both faithful to model computations and human-interpretable
Achieving high interpretability without sacrificing model accuracy
metrics: Quantitative interpretability metrics (not named specifically, but referenced as standard in the field)

results.value: competitive with standard models

novelty_claims: Introduction of the B-cos transformation, which promotes weight-input alignment and enables inherently interpretable DNNs.
B-cos transformation allows any sequence of such layers to be summarised by a single, interpretable linear transformation.
B-cos can be integrated into a wide range of existing architectures, including both CNNs and Vision Transformers, without loss of accuracy.
B-cos explanations outperform other explanation methods under quantitative interpretability metrics and qualitative inspection.
The framework allows for explanations not only of model outputs but also of intermediate representations.
The work extends previous research by integrating normalisation and attention layers into B-cos DNNs and providing broader experimental evaluation.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["ProtoPFaith: a new procedure to extract explanations for trained ProtoPNets using Shapley values on prototype similarity scores","Analysis of ProtoPNet's attribution maps for faithfulness to explanation axioms"],"problems":["Faithfulness of explanations in case-based reasoning neural networks (ProtoPNet)","Violation of explanation axioms by existing attribution maps in ProtoPNet"],"datasets":["CUB-200-2011","Stanford Dogs","RSNA"],"metrics":["Area Over the Perturbation Curve (AOPC)"],"results":[{"metric":"Area Over the Perturbation Curve (AOPC)","value":"ProtoPFaith outperforms ProtoPNet by a factor >10^3 on all experiments"}],"novelty_claims":["Propose ProtoPFaith, a new explanation extraction method for ProtoPNets that uses Shapley values to provide faithful explanations","Demonstrate that ProtoPFaith explanations comply with all established axioms for faithful explanations, unlike previous methods","Quantitatively and qualitatively show the superiority of ProtoPFaith explanations over ProtoPNet attribution maps"]}

methods: ProtoPFaith: a new procedure to extract explanations for trained ProtoPNets using Shapley values on prototype similarity scores
Analysis of ProtoPNet's attribution maps for faithfulness to explanation axioms
problems: Faithfulness of explanations in case-based reasoning neural networks (ProtoPNet)
Violation of explanation axioms by existing attribution maps in ProtoPNet

results.value: ProtoPFaith outperforms ProtoPNet by a factor >10^3 on all experiments

novelty_claims: Propose ProtoPFaith, a new explanation extraction method for ProtoPNets that uses Shapley values to provide faithful explanations

B-cos convolutional neural networks (B-cos CNNs)

novelty_claims: Introduction of the B-cos transform, which promotes weight-input alignment during training to improve interpretability.
B-cos transform allows any sequence of transforms to be summarised by a single linear transform, enabling faithful and interpretable explanations for both output and intermediate neurons.

B-cos networks provide high-quality, highly interpretable explanations that outperform other explanation methods under quantitative interpretability metrics.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["XProtoNet: an interpretable diagnosis framework for chest radiography that learns disease prototypes from X-ray images and provides both global and local explanations","Adaptive prototype learning: prototypes are learned within dynamically predicted occurrence areas for each disease, rather than fixed-size patches","Comparison of input image features with learned prototypes for diagnosis"],"problems":["Lack of accurate and trustworthy explanations in automated deep learning-based diagnosis of chest radiography","Existing methods provide only localization as explanation, not the reasoning process behind predictions","Fixed-size prototype patches in previous case-based models are insufficient for capturing disease features that appear in variable-sized regions"],"datasets":["NIH Chest X-ray dataset"],"metrics":["Classification performance (likely AUC or accuracy, though not explicitly stated)"],"results":[{"metric":"Classification performance (state-of-the-art)","value":"Outperforms other state-of-the-art methods on the NIH Chest X-ray dataset"}],"novelty_claims":["First interpretable model for chest radiography diagnosis that provides both global and local explanations","Novel method for learning disease-representative features within dynamically predicted occurrence areas, rather than fixed-size patches","Improved interpretability and diagnostic performance over previous methods"]}

Improved interpretability and diagnostic performance over previous methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["PANIC (Prototypical Additive Neural Network for Interpretable Classification)","Integration of 3D image and tabular data using a Generalized Additive Model (GAM) framework","Inherently interpretable CNN for 3D image data based on similarity to class-representative prototypes","Separate neural nets for each tabular feature, combined additively"],"problems":["Lack of interpretability in deep learning models for Alzheimer's disease classification","Need for integrating heterogeneous data (3D images and tabular data) for accurate AD diagnosis","Limitations of post-hoc explanation methods for neural networks (low fidelity, only local explanations)","Absence of inherently interpretable heterogeneous DNNs for AD classification"],"datasets":["Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI)"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy (implied by 'state-of-the-art performance')"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["PANIC is the first inherently interpretable deep neural network that integrates both 3D image and tabular data for Alzheimer's disease classification.","PANIC provides both local and global interpretability by design, avoiding the need for post-hoc explanations.","PANIC achieves state-of-the-art performance in AD classification while being fully transparent.","PANIC extracts biologically meaningful signatures of Alzheimer's disease."]}

methods: PANIC (Prototypical Additive Neural Network for Interpretable Classification)
Integration of 3D image and tabular data using a Generalized Additive Model (GAM) framework
Inherently interpretable CNN for 3D image data based on similarity to class-representative prototypes
Separate neural nets for each tabular feature, combined additively
problems: Lack of interpretability in deep learning models for Alzheimer's disease classification
Need for integrating heterogeneous data (3D images and tabular data) for accurate AD diagnosis
Limitations of post-hoc explanation methods for neural networks (low fidelity, only local explanations)
Absence of inherently interpretable heterogeneous DNNs for AD classification
novelty_claims: PANIC is the first inherently interpretable deep neural network that integrates both 3D image and tabular data for Alzheimer's disease classification.
PANIC provides both local and global interpretability by design, avoiding the need for post-hoc explanations.
PANIC achieves state-of-the-art performance in AD classification while being fully transparent.
PANIC extracts biologically meaningful signatures of Alzheimer's disease.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["PIP-Net (Patch-based Intuitive Prototypes Network): an interpretable image classification model that learns prototypical parts in a self-supervised fashion, correlating with human vision","Sparse linear layer connecting learned prototypes to classes with non-negative weights, acting as a scoring sheet","Novel regularization for learning prototype similarity that better aligns with human visual perception","Automatic selection of the number of prototypes (only upper bound specified), promoting sparsity and compact explanations"],"problems":["Existing prototype-based interpretable image classification models learn prototypes that do not align with human visual perception, leading to unintuitive explanations","Semantic gap between learned prototypes in latent space and human-interpretable concepts in pixel space","Lack of semantic correspondence between prototypes and human concepts in current part-prototype models","Existing models require specifying the number of prototypes beforehand and are not compact or sparse","Difficulty in handling out-of-distribution data in interpretable models"],"datasets":["Clipart dataset (with relabeled binary classification task: 'sun OR dog' vs. 'NOT (sun OR dog)')"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy","Sparsity ratio (percentage of unused prototypes)","Correlation of prototypes with ground-truth object parts (semantic alignment)"],"results":[{"metric":"Sparsity ratio","value":">99% (very high sparsity, i.e., very few prototypes used for explanations)"}],"novelty_claims":["PIP-Net is an intrinsically interpretable image classifier that learns prototypes in a self-supervised way, correlating with human vision and closing the semantic gap between latent and pixel space","The model uses a novel regularization for prototype similarity that better aligns with human visual perception","PIP-Net can abstain from making a decision for out-of-distribution data, providing a mechanism to say 'I haven't seen this before'","Unlike previous models, PIP-Net does not require specifying the exact number of prototypes, only an upper bound, and automatically selects a compact set of prototypes for accurate and interpretable classification","PIP-Net provides both global and local explanations that are intuitive, faithful, and semantically meaningful"]}

methods: PIP-Net (Patch-based Intuitive Prototypes Network): an interpretable image classification model that learns prototypical parts in a self-supervised fashion, correlating with human vision
Sparse linear layer connecting learned prototypes to classes with non-negative weights, acting as a scoring sheet
Novel regularization for learning prototype similarity that better aligns with human visual perception
Automatic selection of the number of prototypes (only upper bound specified), promoting sparsity and compact explanations
problems: Existing prototype-based interpretable image classification models learn prototypes that do not align with human visual perception, leading to unintuitive explanations
Semantic gap between learned prototypes in latent space and human-interpretable concepts in pixel space
Lack of semantic correspondence between prototypes and human concepts in current part-prototype models
Existing models require specifying the number of prototypes beforehand and are not compact or sparse
Difficulty in handling out-of-distribution data in interpretable models
datasets: Clipart dataset (with relabeled binary classification task: 'sun OR dog' vs. 'NOT (sun OR dog)')
metrics: Correlation of prototypes with ground-truth object parts (semantic alignment)

results.value: >99% (very high sparsity, i.e., very few prototypes used for explanations)

novelty_claims: PIP-Net is an intrinsically interpretable image classifier that learns prototypes in a self-supervised way, correlating with human vision and closing the semantic gap between latent and pixel space
The model uses a novel regularization for prototype similarity that better aligns with human visual perception
PIP-Net can abstain from making a decision for out-of-distribution data, providing a mechanism to say 'I haven't seen this before'

Unlike previous models, PIP-Net does not require specifying the exact number of prototypes, only an upper bound, and automatically selects a compact set of prototypes for accurate and interpretable classification
PIP-Net provides both global and local explanations that are intuitive, faithful, and semantically meaningful

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Inherently interpretable neural network using case-based reasoning (IAIA-BL)","Prototype-based deep learning architecture for mammography","Incorporation of both coarsely-annotated and finely-annotated data via a novel training scheme","Use of learned prototypical image parts for semantic feature detection","Fine-annotation loss to leverage expert pixel-level annotations"],"problems":["Lack of interpretability in deep learning models for medical imaging","Ensuring model decisions are based on relevant medical information rather than confounding factors","Providing clinically meaningful explanations for mammographic image classification","Improving accuracy and trustworthiness of AI in high-stakes medical settings"],"datasets":["Mammographic images with both whole-image labels and a small set of pixel-level (fine) annotations (collected by the authors' radiology team)"],"metrics":["Accuracy (for mass margin detection and malignancy prediction)"],"results":[{"metric":"Mass margin detection accuracy","value":"Equal or higher than other methods (exact value not specified)"},{"metric":"Explanation detail","value":"More detailed than other methods (qualitative improvement)"}],"novelty_claims":["Development of an inherently interpretable deep learning model (IAIA-BL) for mammographic image classification using case-based reasoning and learned prototypes.","The model provides both predictions and detailed, clinically relevant explanations that mimic radiologist reasoning.","Introduction of a novel training scheme that combines coarsely-annotated and finely-annotated data, including a fine-annotation loss, to improve accuracy and interpretability with limited expert-annotated data.","The approach effectively addresses confounding issues in medical machine learning by ensuring decisions are based on medically relevant features."]}

methods: Inherently interpretable neural network using case-based reasoning (IAIA-BL)
Prototype-based deep learning architecture for mammography
Incorporation of both coarsely-annotated and finely-annotated data via a novel training scheme
Use of learned prototypical image parts for semantic feature detection
Fine-annotation loss to leverage expert pixel-level annotations
problems: Lack of interpretability in deep learning models for medical imaging
Ensuring model decisions are based on relevant medical information rather than confounding factors
Providing clinically meaningful explanations for mammographic image classification
Improving accuracy and trustworthiness of AI in high-stakes medical settings
datasets: Mammographic images with both whole-image labels and a small set of pixel-level (fine) annotations (collected by the authors' radiology team)
metrics: Accuracy (for mass margin detection and malignancy prediction)

results.value: Equal or higher than other methods (exact value not specified)
More detailed than other methods (qualitative improvement)

novelty_claims: Development of an inherently interpretable deep learning model (IAIA-BL) for mammographic image classification using case-based reasoning and learned prototypes.
The model provides both predictions and detailed, clinically relevant explanations that mimic radiologist reasoning.

Introduction of a novel training scheme that combines coarsely-annotated and finely-annotated data, including a fine-annotation loss, to improve accuracy and interpretability with limited expert-annotated data.
The approach effectively addresses confounding issues in medical machine learning by ensuring decisions are based on medically relevant features.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["RISE (Randomized Input Sampling for Explanation): a black-box approach that generates pixel-wise importance maps by probing the model with randomly masked versions of the input image and combining the outputs to estimate pixel saliency."],"problems":["Explainable AI for deep neural networks, specifically explaining image classification decisions of black-box models.","Generating saliency/importance maps for model predictions without access to model internals (true black-box setting).","Evaluating the quality of explanations in a way that reflects the causal impact of input features on model decisions."],"datasets":["PASCAL VOC","ImageNet"],"metrics":["Deletion metric (area under the probability curve as salient pixels are removed)","Insertion metric (rise in class probability as salient pixels are added)","Pointing metric (human-annotated object segments)"],"results":[{"metric":"Deletion AUC (ImageNet example, goldfish class)","value":"RISE achieves the lowest AUC compared to Grad-CAM and LIME, indicating more accurate and causal saliency."},{"metric":"Performance on benchmark datasets (PASCAL VOC, ImageNet)","value":"RISE matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art white-box and black-box explanation methods on automatic and human-centric metrics."}],"novelty_claims":["RISE is a true black-box explanation method that does not require access to model internals (parameters, gradients, or features) and is applicable to any image classification network.","RISE introduces a randomized input sampling approach to generate pixel-wise importance maps, which is conceptually different from mainstream white-box saliency approaches.","The paper proposes two automatic, causal evaluation metrics (deletion and insertion) that do not require human annotation and better assess the causal impact of explanations."]}

methods: RISE (Randomized Input Sampling for Explanation): a black-box approach that generates pixel-wise importance maps by probing the model with randomly masked versions of the input image and combining the outputs to estimate pixel saliency.
problems: Explainable AI for deep neural networks, specifically explaining image classification decisions of black-box models.
Generating saliency/importance maps for model predictions without access to model internals (true black-box setting).
Evaluating the quality of explanations in a way that reflects the causal impact of input features on model decisions.
metrics: Deletion metric (area under the probability curve as salient pixels are removed)
Insertion metric (rise in class probability as salient pixels are added)

results.value: RISE achieves the lowest AUC compared to Grad-CAM and LIME, indicating more accurate and causal saliency.

results.metric: Performance on benchmark datasets (PASCAL VOC, ImageNet)
results.value: RISE matches or exceeds the performance of state-of-the-art white-box and black-box explanation methods on automatic and human-centric metrics.

novelty_claims: RISE is a true black-box explanation method that does not require access to model internals (parameters, gradients, or features) and is applicable to any image classification network.
RISE introduces a randomized input sampling approach to generate pixel-wise importance maps, which is conceptually different from mainstream white-box saliency approaches.
The paper proposes two automatic, causal evaluation metrics (deletion and insertion) that do not require human annotation and better assess the causal impact of explanations.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs): neural networks with an intermediate layer aligned to human-specified concepts, trained with an additional loss to predict concepts before predicting the final label","Intervention on concept predictions at test time: manually correcting predicted concepts to improve final prediction","Variants of training CBMs: exploring different ways of training the concept prediction and label prediction stages"],"problems":["Lack of interpretability and manipulability in standard end-to-end neural networks, which do not support reasoning or intervention in terms of high-level concepts","Tradeoff between predictive accuracy and interpretability in terms of human-understandable concepts","Difficulty in providing counterfactual explanations and supporting human-model interaction in standard models"],"datasets":["Osteoarthritis knee x-ray grading dataset (Nevitt et al., 2006)","Caltech-UCSD Birds-200-2011 (CUB) dataset (Wah et al., 2011)"],"metrics":["Task accuracy (final label prediction accuracy)","Concept accuracy (accuracy of predicted concepts)"],"results":[{"metric":"Task accuracy (CBM vs. end-to-end)","value":"CBMs achieve competitive or even higher accuracy compared to standard end-to-end models on both osteoarthritis grading and bird identification tasks"},{"metric":"Concept accuracy","value":"CBMs attain high concept accuracy, enabling meaningful interventions"},{"metric":"Task accuracy after intervention","value":"Accuracy improves substantially beyond that of a standard model when concept mistakes are corrected at test time"}],"novelty_claims":["Propose a simple and general method to turn any end-to-end neural network into a concept bottleneck model using concept annotations at training time","Demonstrate that concept bottleneck models can achieve competitive accuracy with standard models while enabling direct intervention and interpretation in terms of high-level concepts","Show that interventions on concept predictions at test time can substantially improve model accuracy, supporting richer human-model interaction","Systematically study the trade-offs between task accuracy, concept accuracy, and the ability to intervene, including robustness to covariate shift"]}

methods: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs): neural networks with an intermediate layer aligned to human-specified concepts, trained with an additional loss to predict concepts before predicting the final label
Intervention on concept predictions at test time: manually correcting predicted concepts to improve final prediction
Variants of training CBMs: exploring different ways of training the concept prediction and label prediction stages
problems: Lack of interpretability and manipulability in standard end-to-end neural networks, which do not support reasoning or intervention in terms of high-level concepts
Tradeoff between predictive accuracy and interpretability in terms of human-understandable concepts
Difficulty in providing counterfactual explanations and supporting human-model interaction in standard models
datasets: Osteoarthritis knee x-ray grading dataset (Nevitt et al., 2006)

results.value: CBMs achieve competitive or even higher accuracy compared to standard end-to-end models on both osteoarthritis grading and bird identification tasks
CBMs attain high concept accuracy, enabling meaningful interventions
Accuracy improves substantially beyond that of a standard model when concept mistakes are corrected at test time

novelty_claims: Propose a simple and general method to turn any end-to-end neural network into a concept bottleneck model using concept annotations at training time
Demonstrate that concept bottleneck models can achieve competitive accuracy with standard models while enabling direct intervention and interpretation in terms of high-level concepts
Show that interventions on concept predictions at test time can substantially improve model accuracy, supporting richer human-model interaction
Systematically study the trade-offs between task accuracy, concept accuracy, and the ability to intervene, including robustness to covariate shift

Support set distillation via clustering to create a small distilled support set
Interpretability via label weights from the NW head
Support influence function for quantifying influence of support elements on predictions
problems: Lack of interpretability in parametric deep neural network classification heads

Computational inefficiency of nonparametric methods
Need for explainable and debuggable classification models

results.value: NW head yields better calibration than parametric FC head, especially in data-limited settings
NW head achieves comparable (and sometimes better) accuracy than FC head on a variety of computer vision datasets

novelty_claims: Empirical analysis of a simple, non-learnable, nonparametric Nadaraya-Watson prediction head for neural networks
Proposal of a support set distillation approach using clustering to improve inference efficiency
Introduction of the 'support influence function' as a novel, easy-to-compute metric for interpretability and debugging

Demonstration that the NW head provides better calibrated and more interpretable predictions compared to standard parametric heads, especially in small to medium-sized fine-grained classification tasks

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["BagNet: a deep neural network architecture inspired by bag-of-features (BoF) models, based on a variant of ResNet-50, which classifies images using counts of small local features without considering their spatial arrangement"],"problems":["Improving interpretability of deep neural network (DNN) decisions in image classification","Understanding the decision-making process of DNNs on complex perceptual tasks","Bridging the gap between performance and interpretability in DNNs"],"datasets":["ImageNet"],"metrics":["Top-5 accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Top-5 accuracy (33x33 px features)","value":"87.6%"},{"metric":"Top-5 accuracy (17x17 px features)","value":"AlexNet performance (approx. 80%)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of BagNet, a DNN architecture that combines the interpretability of bag-of-features models with the performance of modern DNNs","Demonstration that BagNet achieves high accuracy on ImageNet using only local features and no spatial relationships","Empirical evidence that BagNets' decision-making is similar to state-of-the-art DNNs, suggesting that recent DNN improvements are due to better fine-tuning rather than fundamentally different decision strategies","BagNet provides a model that is easier to analyze and interpret by design, facilitating understanding of how image parts influence classification"]}

methods: BagNet: a deep neural network architecture inspired by bag-of-features (BoF) models, based on a variant of ResNet-50, which classifies images using counts of small local features without considering their spatial arrangement
problems: Improving interpretability of deep neural network (DNN) decisions in image classification
Understanding the decision-making process of DNNs on complex perceptual tasks
Bridging the gap between performance and interpretability in DNNs

results.value: AlexNet performance (approx. 80%)

novelty_claims: Introduction of BagNet, a DNN architecture that combines the interpretability of bag-of-features models with the performance of modern DNNs
Demonstration that BagNet achieves high accuracy on ImageNet using only local features and no spatial relationships

Empirical evidence that BagNets' decision-making is similar to state-of-the-art DNNs, suggesting that recent DNN improvements are due to better fine-tuning rather than fundamentally different decision strategies
BagNet provides a model that is easier to analyze and interpret by design, facilitating understanding of how image parts influence classification

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["WeightAlign: normalizing activations by normalizing filter weights using mean and scaled standard deviation computed within a filter, without using sample statistics"],"problems":["Instability of batch normalization (BN) for small batch sizes due to reliance on mini-batch sample statistics","Unreliable or unavailable sample statistics in applications such as domain adaptation, high-resolution images, and small batch training","Less stable performance of alternative normalization methods (Instance Norm, Layer Norm, Group Norm) that depend on single-sample statistics"],"datasets":["CIFAR-10","CIFAR-100","ImageNet","PASCAL VOC 2012","Office-31"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy","Semantic segmentation performance (e.g., mIoU)","Domain adaptation accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Classification accuracy on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet","value":"State-of-the-art performance (exact values not specified in provided content)"},{"metric":"Semantic segmentation performance on PASCAL VOC 2012","value":"State-of-the-art performance (exact values not specified in provided content)"},{"metric":"Domain adaptation accuracy on Office-31","value":"State-of-the-art performance (exact values not specified in provided content)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes WeightAlign, a method for activation normalization that does not require any sample statistics","Normalizes activations by re-parameterizing filter weights using weight statistics (mean and scaled standard deviation within a filter)","Performance is independent of batch size and stable across a wide range of batch sizes","WeightAlign is orthogonal to sample statistics and can be combined with any activation normalization method","Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across five datasets in classification, segmentation, and domain adaptation"]}

methods: WeightAlign: normalizing activations by normalizing filter weights using mean and scaled standard deviation computed within a filter, without using sample statistics
problems: Instability of batch normalization (BN) for small batch sizes due to reliance on mini-batch sample statistics
Unreliable or unavailable sample statistics in applications such as domain adaptation, high-resolution images, and small batch training
Less stable performance of alternative normalization methods (Instance Norm, Layer Norm, Group Norm) that depend on single-sample statistics

results.value: State-of-the-art performance (exact values not specified in provided content)

results.metric: Semantic segmentation performance on PASCAL VOC 2012
results.value: State-of-the-art performance (exact values not specified in provided content)
State-of-the-art performance (exact values not specified in provided content)

novelty_claims: Proposes WeightAlign, a method for activation normalization that does not require any sample statistics
Normalizes activations by re-parameterizing filter weights using weight statistics (mean and scaled standard deviation within a filter)
Performance is independent of batch size and stable across a wide range of batch sizes
WeightAlign is orthogonal to sample statistics and can be combined with any activation normalization method
Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across five datasets in classification, segmentation, and domain adaptation

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Weight-Feature Alignment (WFA): aligning point features with the principal axes of the network weights to construct a local Invariant Reference Frame (IRF)"],"problems":["Lack of generalizable rotation-invariant learning for 3D point clouds, especially in open and large-range scenes","Current rotation-invariant methods' reliance on global scene/background, limiting applicability to open scenes","Pose ambiguity and non-interpretable orientation in previous IRF-based methods"],"datasets":["Point clouds of single objects","Point clouds of open large-range scenes"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance gap between rotation invariance learning and normal methods","value":"Almost bridged (WFA achieves performance close to normal methods)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Weight-Feature Alignment (WFA), a general solution for robust rotation-invariant feature learning applicable to point clouds from arbitrary scenes, including open and large-range outdoor scenes","WFA aligns point features with the principal axes of network weights, eliminating the effect of orientation and background, and making the activation a function only of the local pattern","Provides a theoretical analysis for the mathematical interpretability of the method","Demonstrates that WFA bridges the performance gap between rotation-invariant and normal methods on both single-object and open-scene point clouds"]}

methods: Weight-Feature Alignment (WFA): aligning point features with the principal axes of the network weights to construct a local Invariant Reference Frame (IRF)
problems: Lack of generalizable rotation-invariant learning for 3D point clouds, especially in open and large-range scenes
Current rotation-invariant methods' reliance on global scene/background, limiting applicability to open scenes
Pose ambiguity and non-interpretable orientation in previous IRF-based methods
datasets: Point clouds of open large-range scenes

results.metric: Performance gap between rotation invariance learning and normal methods
results.value: Almost bridged (WFA achieves performance close to normal methods)

novelty_claims: Proposes Weight-Feature Alignment (WFA), a general solution for robust rotation-invariant feature learning applicable to point clouds from arbitrary scenes, including open and large-range outdoor scenes
WFA aligns point features with the principal axes of network weights, eliminating the effect of orientation and background, and making the activation a function only of the local pattern
Provides a theoretical analysis for the mathematical interpretability of the method
Demonstrates that WFA bridges the performance gap between rotation-invariant and normal methods on both single-object and open-scene point clouds
